title: Why do bug fixes lead to constant response calls (CRs)? A personal reflection on the SpecKit architecture design.
description: A record of encountering consecutive CRs while fixing bugs in SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI): reviewing implicit coupling in the system and discussing personal views on the ideal architecture design for future evolution.
content:
TL;DRWhile fixing a bug that caused SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI) command calls to fail, I encountered a series of rejections (CRs) from Copilot. Upon review, I discovered that this wasn't simply a code error, but rather an implicit coupling in the architecture caused by the system's lack of a "single source of truth." Although the current PR only aims to fix the inconsistencies, this experience has served as an opportunity to deduce the future evolution of a "unified domain core" architecture and share some personal design philosophies.

I've recently spent a lot of time dealing with a seemingly minor issue: on SpecKit (formerly Codex CLI), users can't invoke commands as smoothly as with other agents to trigger a fixed, quick workflow.

My initial assessment of the problem was quite simplistic: it should be a localized fix. Adjusting the naming conventions, generation logic, and calling methods, adding some tests, and that should be about it.

The result was completely different.

During the revision process, Copilot Review kept providing new feedback reports. One would be fixed, and another would pop up; a seemingly minor change would eventually lead to a whole chain of issues involving presets, extensions, hooks, release packaging, and Kimi compatibility migrations. The pressure was immense, and it was extremely energy-consuming.

The problem was eventually solved, and the PRs subsided. But the most valuable part of this period wasn't actually "fixing the bug," but rather using the process to clearly see some real architectural issues with the codebase.

This article aims to share a retrospective analysis of the restoration process.

Starting point: A seemingly small problem

The initial question was very specific:

The experience of invoking quick workflows via commands in the SpecKit terminal is incorrect.
Users expect to quickly enter a standardized workflow through a skill-like approach; however, in the existing implementation, the naming, generation, and invocation methods are not fully aligned, resulting in inconsistent experiences and, in some cases, failing to correctly match the actual output.

If we only look at the "functional points", this seems like a minor tweak to the naming or generation logic.

But once you start digging deeper, you'll find that this problem doesn't just belong to one function.

Because this capability is no longer simply "a file generator." It's connected to:

  • agent configuration
  • Mapping from command to skill
  • Preset installation and uninstallation
  • extension auto-registration
  • Hook call hints
  • GitHub release packaging script
  • Localuv run specifyDifferences from remote release packages
  • Kimi's historical compatibility and migration logic

In other words, you think you're fixing a button, but you're actually touching a distributed protocol system.

Why does Copilot keep getting CR?

The most stressful part of this process is not the difficulty of writing the code, but the fact that Copilot will constantly come up with new code responses.

At first, a natural reaction might be: Have I missed too much? Is the review bot "smarter" than me?

Later, my own judgment changed to another expression:

It's not that it's smarter, but that it looks at the problem from the perspective of "consistency of the entire PR", while I was initially more focused on "fixing the problem according to the current thread".

These two perspectives are very different.

Many of the things I did back then were essentially:

  • Fix an issue that has been pointed out in an existing comment.
  • Pass the current test
  • Ensure that the behavior is valid in this part.

But Copilot is looking at something else entirely:

  • Is this change consistent with the conventions of other modules?
  • Are production and consumption symmetrical?
  • Are the naming conventions consistent across all entry points?
  • Is the deletion, restoration, and migration a complete closed loop?
  • Are the local logic and release artifacts consistent?

This is precisely why many problems manifest themselves in this way:

  • The code itself looks correct.
  • The test also passed.
  • But when placed within the entire supply chain, it will drift.

What I truly felt this time was:

Many CRs are not "implementation errors", but rather "system invariants are not expressed uniformly".

The root cause of the stress is not just high CR.

To be honest, the pressure of this round of review is immense.

Because it's not the kind of pressure where you "you wrote an if statement incorrectly" and then it's over after you fix it, but rather:

  • You fixed a problem
  • The review then points out another inconsistency from another entry point.
  • You might start to doubt whether your understanding of the big picture is complete.
  • Each push may trigger a new thread.

This feeling is very different from simply fixing bugs. It's more like patching holes in the dark in a system without a clear center.

Looking back, what really put pressure on me wasn't the number of comments themselves, but rather what it revealed:

I initially underestimated the discreteness of this business logic.

I thought I was fixing a feature, but in reality I was dealing with a network of rules scattered across multiple modules, scripts, and output chains.

Use a diagram to understand the current skill structure.

The most impactful aspect for me this time was:
The problem isn't in a single function, but rather that the "production-consumption-recovery-migration" process in the entire skill chain is quite fragmented.

flowchart TB
  subgraph Producers["Skill 产物生产端"]
    InitPy["Python: install_ai_skills()"]
    CmdReg["Python: CommandRegistrar.render_skill_command()"]
    ExtReg["Python: ExtensionManager._register_extension_skills()"]
    PresetReg["Python: PresetManager._register_skills()"]
    ReleaseSH["Bash: create-release-packages.sh"]
    ReleasePS["PowerShell: create-release-packages.ps1"]
  end

  subgraph Artifact["核心产物"]
    SkillFile["<skills_dir>/<skill_name>/SKILL.md"]
  end

  subgraph Consumers["Skill 产物消费端"]
    Hook["Hook invocation / hook message"]
    ExtRemove["Extension remove / cleanup"]
    PresetRemove["Preset remove / restore"]
    Migration["Legacy migration"]
  end

  InitPy --> SkillFile
  CmdReg --> SkillFile
  ExtReg --> SkillFile
  PresetReg --> SkillFile
  ReleaseSH --> SkillFile
  ReleasePS --> SkillFile

  SkillFile --> Hook
  SkillFile --> ExtRemove
  SkillFile --> PresetRemove
  SkillFile --> Migration

The most important thing about this chart isn't the number of lines, but rather the fact it illustrates:

The sameSKILL.mdThere are many producers and many consumers.

This means:

  • Changing only one generation logic does not mean that all other generation logics must be changed accordingly.
  • Changing only one consumption logic does not mean that other restore/delete logics will understand the same rules.
  • Just because your local Python installation is fixed doesn't mean the GitHub release package is correct.
  • Any sync issue between Kimi, Codex, extension, preset, and hook will cause drift.

This is why CRs kept popping up in this PR.
You think you're fixing a single point, but you're actually touching a whole network.

Why does a small change trigger a series of CR (Crash Results) events?

If we abstract this problem into a single sentence, it would be:

One rule is that there is no single source of truth.

For example, the "skill naming rules" are not a unified interface in the current architecture, but are scattered in many places.

flowchart LR
  Change["修改一条 skill 规则<br/>例如：speckit.plan -> speckit-plan"]

  A["__init__.py<br/>init / migration"]
  B["agents.py<br/>command -> skill 渲染"]
  C["extensions.py<br/>extension 注册 / hook"]
  D["presets.py<br/>preset 覆盖 / restore"]
  E["release .sh<br/>远程包生成"]
  F["release .ps1<br/>远程包生成"]

  Change --> A
  Change --> B
  Change --> C
  Change --> D
  Change --> E
  Change --> F

The intuitive feeling this structure evokes is:

  • The changes are much larger than they appear.
  • It's easy to miss making changes.
  • Even if one part is overlooked, the system may still appear to be functioning, but the review will quickly identify and pinpoint it.
  • If the test is not written as an "invariant", it is difficult to find the problem in one go.

Copilot kept giving CRs this time not because it was always "nitpicking," but because this type of architecture naturally exposes "inconsistencies."

Several core issues exposed this time

1. `skill` is not a first-class object, but rather a set of string conventions.

The most obvious problem this time is that the concept of skill has not been modeled as a stable domain object.

It's more like a set of agreements scattered in various places:

  • How to name the directory
  • in frontmatternameHow to write
  • How to render the invocation when a hook hint is received?
  • How to restore preset when it is removed?
  • How to generate during extension install?
  • How to package in the release script?

Many of these rules are not expressed through a unified interface, but are implemented separately through string concatenation, path conventions, and YAML assembly.

The result is:

If you change the naming rules for skills, it will almost certainly affect the entire skill chain.

2. Production and consumption are not from a single source.

I went back and checked it again later, and only then did I fully understand the problem:

  • Python code will generate skills locally.
  • shell / PowerShell 的 release 脚本也会生成 skill
  • 默认 specify init 优先又是走 GitHub release 包

这意味着什么？

意味着你“本地代码修对了”，不代表用户“默认拿到的是对的”。

这是这次一个特别典型的坑：
本地 --offline 生成的内容是对的，但远程 release 包里的 Kimi skill 元数据还是旧的，结果用户默认初始化出来就是不一致的。

这种问题如果系统没有单一真源，真的很难靠直觉发现。

3. 生命周期逻辑是分散的

如果一个系统只负责“生成”，那很多问题都还没那么明显。

但 skill 这里不是只生成一次就完了，它还涉及完整生命周期：

  • init 时生成
  • extension 安装时注册
  • preset 安装时覆盖
  • hook 消费时提示调用
  • remove 时恢复或删除
  • 兼容历史命名时迁移

这些动作分别散落在不同文件里，彼此之间靠隐式约定配合。

一旦没有统一模型，就很容易出现这种问题：

  • create 的逻辑改了
  • remove 没跟着改
  • restore 仍然按旧规则理解
  • hook 还在提示旧名字

这种 bug 很顽固，因为单看每个函数都“有道理”，但系统拼起来就不对。

4. migration 只是“挪目录”，不是“语义迁移”

这次一个非常典型的例子，是 Kimi 的历史 skill 迁移。

目录名已经从旧格式迁到了新格式，但 SKILL.md 里的 frontmatter.name 没改。于是出现一种非常别扭的状态：

  • 目录是新的
  • 元数据还是旧的

这类问题说明 migration 只做了文件系统层面的移动，没有做 artifact 语义层面的规范化。

这在早期可能是一个够用的折中，但在系统逐渐复杂之后，就会变成长期负债。

这次修复最终解决了什么

虽然过程很折腾，但最终还是把主要问题都收敛掉了。

包括：

  • Codex 和 Kimi 的 skill naming 对齐
  • hook invocation 和真实 skill 目录对齐
  • extension 自动注册 skill 的 placeholder/path 解析补齐
  • preset remove 时的 restore / cleanup 对称性修复
  • Kimi 在 native skill 模式下和 ai_skills 开关语义对齐
  • 一批围绕 malformed config、非 dict init-options、缺失 SKILL.md 的边界行为补强

从结果上看，这些问题都不是“单点报错修复”，而是在逐步把整条 skill 链路拉回一致状态。

更重要的收获：以后不能只按 thread 修

这次之后，我一个非常明确的想法是：

以后处理这种 PR，不能再只按 review thread 被动修。

必须先做一轮完整的本地审计，先回答下面这些问题：

  • 同一个概念是不是有多套命名规则
  • create / restore / unregister / remove 是否对称
  • hook 渲染和真实产物是否一致
  • 本地生成逻辑和 release 产物是否一致
  • Python、bash、PowerShell 是否各自维护了一套业务规则
  • 测试是在验证新行为，还是还在锁旧行为

如果不先这样审，而是直接跟着已有评论改，很容易陷入一种局部最优、全局继续漂移的状态。

探讨：如果做一次架构整改，目标应该是什么

既然现在的系统存在这么多隐式耦合，如果未来往更合理的方向收敛，我认为 skill 这套逻辑（个人观点）理想的形态应该长成下面这样：

flowchart TB
  subgraph App["应用层 / 用例层"]
    InitSvc["InitService"]
    ExtSvc["ExtensionService"]
    PresetSvc["PresetService"]
    HookSvc["HookService"]
    ReleaseSvc["ReleaseBuildService"]
  end

  subgraph Domain["统一领域核心"]
    SkillSpec["SkillArtifact / SkillSpec"]
    Naming["SkillNamingPolicy"]
    Render["SkillRenderer"]
    Parse["SkillParser"]
    Validate["SkillInvariantValidator"]
    Registry["SkillRegistry / Ownership Model"]
  end

  subgraph Adapters["适配层"]
    FS["Filesystem Adapter"]
    GH["GitHub Release Builder"]
    Shell["Bash / PowerShell Wrapper"]
  end

  App --> Domain
  InitSvc --> SkillSpec
  ExtSvc --> SkillSpec
  PresetSvc --> SkillSpec
  HookSvc --> Naming
  ReleaseSvc --> SkillSpec

  SkillSpec --> Naming
  SkillSpec --> Render
  SkillSpec --> Parse
  SkillSpec --> Validate
  SkillSpec --> Registry

  Render --> FS
  ReleaseSvc --> GH
  Shell --> ReleaseSvc

  FS --> SkillFile["<skills_dir>/<skill_name>/SKILL.md"]
  GH --> ReleaseAsset["release asset / template zip"]

这张图背后的核心思想只有一句话：

skill 应该先是一个领域对象，然后才是一个文件。

也就是说：

  • 命名规则归 SkillNamingPolicy
  • frontmatter/body 渲染归 SkillRenderer
  • 解析和校验归 SkillParser / SkillInvariantValidator
  • ownership 和 source-of-truth 归 Registry
  • shell / PowerShell 不再自己实现业务规则，只做 adapter

这样做之后，SKILL.md 不再是控制平面本身，而只是领域对象的一个落盘结果。

假想的改造对比：修改一条规则时的感受差别

当前状态

flowchart LR
  Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"]
  Rule --> A["init"]
  Rule --> B["preset"]
  Rule --> C["extension"]
  Rule --> D["hook"]
  Rule --> E["bash release"]
  Rule --> F["powershell release"]

直观感受是：

改一条规则，像在同时拆六个炸弹。

理想中的整改后状态

flowchart LR
  Rule["修改 skill 命名 / frontmatter / path 规则"]
  Rule --> Core["Skill Domain Core"]
  Core --> A["init"]
  Core --> B["preset"]
  Core --> C["extension"]
  Core --> D["hook"]
  Core --> E["release builder"]

直观感受会变成：

改一条规则，先改中心，再让外围复用。

这两种感觉差别非常大。

前者是“处处可能漏”；
后者是真正想要达到的目标——“默认不会漂”。

为什么这对 extension 模式特别重要

这次复盘之后，我一个很强的判断是：

SpecKit 已经不只是一个脚手架 CLI 了，它正在长成一个带扩展生态的平台。

而一旦平台化，extension 模式就会不断放大以下问题：

  • skill naming 是否全局统一
  • release 产物和本地逻辑是否一致
  • hook invocation 是否稳定命中真实产物
  • preset / extension 的覆盖和恢复是否对称
  • catalog / priority / ownership 是否可解释、可审计、可回滚

如果这些规则继续分散在：

  • Python 模块
  • Bash 脚本
  • PowerShell 脚本
  • 文件系统约定
  • frontmatter 元数据

那么每增加一个 agent、每增加一种 extension 玩法，复杂度都会继续上升。

反过来，如果先把这套领域层收拢好，后面的扩展能力就会更健康。

这次最真实的感受

如果要用一句更主观的话总结这次经历，我会这么说：

当前架构下，改 skill 相关代码像是在一张旧地图上修路；
如果某天完成了上述的领域建模整改，才会更像是在真正的交通系统上加规则。

前者靠经验、靠补丁、靠小心；
后者才是我心目中能真正支撑 extension 生态健康发展的正确形态。

写在最后

这次修复让我挺直观地感受到一件事：

很多让人有压力的 review，并不是因为你不会修 bug，而是因为系统本身没有把规则收拢好。

当业务逻辑足够离散，任何改动都会天然带着放大器。
这时 review 不再只是“找错”，而更像是在提醒你：这个系统里还有太多隐式耦合。

从结果看，这次问题最后都填平了。
但对我来说，更有价值的是借这个复盘过程，看见了真正该补的东西，其实不仅仅是眼前的 bug，更是下一步架构设计中需要具备的表达能力。

如果这篇文章关于“理想架构”的个人探讨能给同样在维护复杂 CLI、插件系统、工作流系统的人一点参考，那这段折腾也算值了。

推荐阅读

如果你对 SpecKit 或系统重构演进感兴趣，推荐阅读本站的其他相关文章：

  • SpecKit 结构化文档驱动（SDD）设计与实践
  • 复杂 CLI 的演进之路：基于前端重构底层基础设施
